headline: Semis Hammered as Hormuz Fears Lift Defensives; Breadth Leans Negative at Midday
body_html:
Tuesday's mid-session picture is one of sharp divergence: defensive sectors are holding the tape together while cyclicals and semiconductors absorb heavy selling. Of 396 names tracked, 216 are trading lower versus 177 higher, a negative lean that underscores how selectively buyers are deploying capital. Healthcare leads all sectors, up 0.82%, with Consumer and Software close behind at 0.65% and 0.60%, respectively. At the other end, Semiconductors are down more than five percent as a group — the worst reading across the board — with Industrials off 2.17% and Materials sliding 1.41%.

The dominant theme mid-session is geopolitical risk in the Strait of Hormuz. Reports of missile strikes on tankers near the strait, with Qatar pointing blame at Iran, have rattled energy-supply confidence even as Saudi Arabia moved to cut crude prices — a discount that traders say is finding few takers among already-sated Asian buyers. That backdrop is keeping defensives bid while pressuring industrial and capital-equipment names exposed to global trade disruption.

DoorDash stands out as the session's clear standout mover, up just over five percent intraday. Into the close, watch whether the semiconductor complex stabilizes after Applied Materials and several peers posted losses approaching or exceeding eight percent — any further deterioration there could drag the broader tape meaningfully lower.

briefs.headline: Tanker Strikes Near Hormuz Rattle Energy Markets
briefs.body: Two tankers, including a Saudi-flagged vessel, sustained damage near the Strait of Hormuz following reports of Iranian missile fire, sources say. Qatar has publicly attributed responsibility to Iran. NATO allies are now said to be convening discussions around Hormuz tensions and a potential coordinated maritime mission with Gulf Arab partners.

briefs.headline: Semiconductor Sector Bears the Brunt of Tuesday Selling
briefs.body: The semiconductor group is down more than five percent as a cohort at midday, the weakest sector in our universe by a wide margin. Applied Materials has shed 9.44%, while Alpha and Omega Semiconductor and Onto Innovation are each off more than eight percent. Equipment and test-gear names are broadly under pressure.

briefs.headline: DoorDash Surges More Than Five Percent, Leads All Gainers
briefs.body: DoorDash is the standout mover of the session, advancing 5.02% through midday to top the gainers list. No company from the broader universe comes close to matching that gain intraday. The move lifts the Internet-Delivery name to the head of an otherwise cautious tape.

briefs.headline: Pharma Names Anchor Healthcare Leadership
briefs.body: Johnson & Johnson and Eli Lilly are each adding more than 3.4% intraday, helping Healthcare claim the top sector slot with a gain of 0.82%. The two Pharma-Medtech names rank among the top five gainers in the entire universe at this hour, drawing rotation flows away from higher-beta technology and industrial positions.

briefs.headline: Saudi Price Cut Fails to Stir Asian Crude Demand
briefs.body: Saudi Arabia moved to reduce crude oil prices in a bid to stimulate demand, but traders say the discount is unlikely to persuade Asian buyers who are already well-supplied. Separately, sources indicate Riyadh is evaluating an expansion of its oil pipeline network toward the Red Sea, potentially offering an alternative export route around Hormuz.

briefs.headline: Shell Lifts Gas Output Outlook Ahead of Q2 Results
briefs.body: Shell raised its second-quarter natural gas production guidance and signaled stronger-than-expected results from its gas trading operations. The update provides a measure of upside clarity for the integrated energy major ahead of formal quarterly reporting, arriving as Hormuz-related supply concerns add a fresh layer of volatility to global energy markets.
